title: CNF Certification Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of CNF Certification with 25 questions.

questions.question: Design an end-to-end secure CNF config-update pipeline in a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster where CNFs pull from a central ConfigRepo. The pipeline must (1) sign payloads with tenant KMS-backed keys and attach SPDX SBOM, (2) enforce via a validating webhook that only signed, SBOM-attested configs are applied, and (3) include a runtime sidecar that revalidates on startup and refresh. Provide minimal YAML policy snippets and a one-namespace test manifest to validate in isolation?

options.text: Design a per-tenant config-signing flow: a ConfigSigner signs JSON payloads with tenant KMS keys, appends an SPDX SBOM, and stores the signature in an annotation. A ValidatingWebhook rejects unsigned

Implement a comprehensive upgrade governance framework that validates CNF upgrades through image provenance verification and runtime attestation before tenant deployment. Enforce Cosign-signed images with tenant-specifi…

Implement per-tenant canary routing inside the CNF sidecar by reading the X-TENANT-ID header and a per-namespace ConfigMap containing cnf.canary.enabled and cnf.canary.sample-rate (0-100). If enabled, compute a determin…

Design a comprehensive runtime attestation gate for CNFs in a multi-tenant Kubernetes platform that performs post-deployment verification against a central attestation service. The system computes a per-tenant trust sco…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses end-to-end security for CNF config delivery: signing, SBOM provenance, admission controls, and runtime validation in a multi-tenant environment.

## Key Concepts
- Tenant-specific key management and signing
- SPDX SBOM attachment and verification
- ValidatingWebhookConfiguration and immutable annotations
- Runtime sidecar for periodic revalidation and hot reload

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal ValidatingWebhookConfiguration (illustrative, non-exhaustive)
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: cnf-config-signing-webhook
webhooks:
  - name: cnf-config-signing.example.com
    clientConfig:
      service:
        name: config-signer
        namespace: default
    rules:
      - apiGroups: ["v1"]
        apiVersions: ["v1"]
        operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
        resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
```
```yaml
# Minimal test manifest (illustrative)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: tenant-config
  annotations:
    cnf.sig: "<signature>"
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would key rotation and revocation be propagated across tenants?
- How would you test the impact of revocation on running CNFs and rollback paths?

questions.question: Advanced CNF certification: In a multi-tenant CNF gateway on Kubernetes, implement a per-tenant pluggable authentication policy using a TenantAuthPolicy CRD that supports OIDC, API Key, and mTLS. The gateway sidecar must fetch policy, attempt methods in order until one succeeds, rotate credentials via a per-tenant Secret, and provide a minimal one-namespace manifest, CRD, and a validation test. How would you design and implement this?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This tests end-to-end multi-tenant auth design with pluggable methods and policy-driven routing.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes CRD design, dynamic policy loading
- Sidecar pattern, per-tenant caching
- Authentication backends: OIDC, API Key, MTLS
- Secret management and token rotation

## Code Example
```javascript
# TenantAuthPolicy CRD, simplified
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: TenantAuthPolicy
metadata:
  name: tenant-a
spec:
  allowedMethods:
    - OIDC
    - APIKey
    - MTLS
  defaultMethod: OIDC
  methodOrder:
    - OIDC
    - APIKey
    - MTLS
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle policy revocation and sudden tenant isolation?
- How would you test rotation without disrupting live traffic?

questions.question: In a multi-tenant CNF gateway platform on Kubernetes with federated control planes across regions, design a policy-driven egress control system that hot-reloads per-tenant rules to gateways within 2 seconds, deterministically resolves conflicts, and survives control-plane outages via a last-known-good state. Include: data model for TenantPolicy CRD, distribution mechanism (watch/stream), conflict resolution strategy, and minimal YAML to validate in one namespace?

Design TenantPolicy CRD with fields: tenantID, version, rules (dest, port, protocol, action). Central policy store synchronized across regions; gateways subscribe via gRPC stream and apply delta updat

Design a tenant-scoped CRD that encodes route, canaryWeight, rolloutDuration, and rollbackOnFailure. A central, federated control plane publishes Envoy xDS updates to gateway sidecars via Aggregated Discovery Service (A…

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design scalable, real-time policy distribution across multi-region CNF gateways, with strong conflict resolution and fault tolerance.

## Key Concepts

- CRD design for per-tenant policies
- Real-time delta propagation via streaming
- Conflict resolution using versioning
- Outage resilience with last-known-good cache

## Code Example

```yaml
# TenantPolicy CRD (sketch)
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: TenantPolicy
metadata:
  name: sample
spec:
  tenantID: tenant-A
  version: 42
  rules:
    - dest: 10.0.0.0/24
      port: 443
      protocol: tcp
      action: allow
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test the 2-second propagation under load?
- How would you handle tenant policy revocation scenarios?

questions.question: You're deploying a CNF-based API gateway across 2 Kubernetes clusters. A recent upgrade causes a cross-tenant data bleed under load due to a shared in-memory cache. Outline a beginner-friendly, end-to-end plan to fix and roll out safely: (a) reproduce in staging with two tenants and isolated traffic, (b) implement tenant-scoped cache keys and per-tenant isolation checks, (c) add unit/integration tests for isolation, (d) perform blue/green canary rollout with tenant-based traffic splits, (e) observability: per-tenant SLA metrics and automatic rollback if bleed is detected. Include a minimal code snippet for tenant-scoped cache key?

Reproduce with two tenants in separate namespaces, enable per-tenant cache keys (tenant_id + resource_id), add a small unit test asserting no cross-tenant data is returned, roll out a blue/green canar
Configure Cosign signing with a KMS-backed key, generate an SPDX SBOM and annotate the image with its timestamp, then enforce via Gatekeeper/OPA constraint requiring both signature and SBOM freshness

Design a scalable, four-region CNF gateway provenance flow: sign images with tenant KMS keys via Cosign, attach per-tenant SBOMs, publish Rekor entries, and enforce with Gatekeeper/OPA. Include runtim

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question probes practical skills in tenant isolation, cache safety, and controlled rollouts, plus test design and observability.

## Key Concepts
- Multi-tenant isolation
- Tenant-scoped caching
- Unit/integration tests for isolation
- Blue/green canary rollouts with traffic splitting
- Observability and automatic rollback

## Code Example
```javascript
function cacheKey(tenantId, resourceId) {
  return `${tenantId}:${resourceId}`;
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you monitor cross-tenant bleed in production?
- How would you adapt the canary ratio if bleed indicators appear?

Provide minimal YAML: a NamespaceConfigMap cnf.tracing.enabled, cnf.tracing.endpoint, a sidecar snippet to mount and configure OTLP, and a one-namespace manifest to validate end-to-end tracing?

Describe how to attach an OpenTelemetry sidecar to the CNF gateway that tags every span with tenantID from pod labels and exports traces to a Jaeger collector via OTLP. Include: a NamespaceConfigMap c

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical understanding of multi-tenant tracing integration using OpenTelemetry in CNF gateways.

## Key Concepts
- OpenTelemetry in Kubernetes
- OTLP exporter to Jaeger
- Per-tenant resource attributes
- Downward API and sidecar pattern
- Basic trace verification

## Code Example
```yaml
# NamespaceConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cnf-tracing-config
data:
  cnf.tracing.enabled: "true"
  cnf.tracing.endpoint: "jaeger-collector.default.svc.cluster.local:4317"
  cnf.tracing.sample-rate: "0.5"
```

```bash
# Sidecar env injection (conceptual)
env:
- name: TENANT_ID
  valueFrom:
    fieldRef:
      fieldPath: metadata.labels['cnf-tenant-id']
- name: OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES
  value: service.name=cnf-gateway,tenant_id=${TENANT_ID}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle tenant ID rotation without restart?
- How to isolate tenants if Jaeger stores grow too large?

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a dynamic, per-tenant policy engine integrated into the data plane using WASM, not a static filter.

## Key Concepts
- WASM mutator in a CNF gateway
- Per-tenant isolation via header normalization and path rewrite
- Dynamic policy from Namespace ConfigMap and hot reload
- In-memory caching and zero-downtime updates

```yaml

```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test policy conflicts between tenants and handle rollback?
- What are the security implications of mutating requests in-band and how would you audit changes?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design per-tenant safety controls and automation, not just static configs. It touches runtime remediation, Prometheus-based signals, and Kubernetes operators, with clear config via NamespaceConfigMap.

## Key Concepts

- per-tenant isolation and remediation policy
- Prometheus-based signal with restart metrics
- idempotent remediation actions and anti-flapping
- Kubernetes ConfigMaps, RBAC, and operator design basics

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cnf.remediation
  namespace: tenant-a
data:
  enabled: "true"
  threshold: "3"
  window: "600"
  action: "rollingRestart"
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you test for anti-flap behavior under bursty traffic?
- What metrics would you surface in a dashboard to validate per-tenant remediation health?

Provide minimal YAML and a one-namespace manifest to validate end-to-end?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Question Matters
This question evaluates practical, day-2 operational skills for implementing per-tenant rate limiting in CNF gateways—a common requirement in multi-tenant Kubernetes environments.

## Key Concepts
- Namespace-scoped configuration for feature toggles
- Sidecar pattern for tenant isolation
- Redis-based rate limiting with sliding window TTL and 429 responses

## Code Example
```go
func allow(tenant, path string) bool {
  key := tenant + ":" + pathHash(path)
  count, _ := redis.Incr(key).Result()
  if count == 1 { redis.Expire(key, time.Minute) }
  return count <= 100
}
```

## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to implement tenant-scoped routing logic driven by request headers, without pod restarts.

## Key Concepts
- Header-based routing
- Tenant isolation
- Sidecar routing logic
- Namespace ConfigMap for routing rules
- Graceful fallback for missing/unsupported versions

## Code Example

```javascript
// Pseudo routing map (conceptual)
const routes = {
  'tenantA': { 'v1': 'tenantA-backend-v1', 'v2': 'tenantA-backend-v2' }
};
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test the fallback when X-TENANT-ID is missing?
- How would you monitor per-tenant version usage and errors?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests the ability to implement distributed tracing in a multi-tenant CNF environment, focusing on tenant-scoped attributes, context propagation, and config-driven enablement.

## Key Concepts
- OpenTelemetry instrumentation in gateway and sidecar
- W3C trace context propagation across services
- Tenant-scoped attributes: tenantID and path
- Namespace ConfigMap controls: cnf.trace.enabled and cnf.trace.sample-rate
- End-to-end validation with minimal YAML manifests

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cnf.trace
  namespace: default
data:
  cnf.trace.enabled: "true"
  cnf.trace.sample-rate: "0.5"
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle sampling decisions per-tenant if tenants have different SLAs?
- How would you verify trace completeness when downstream CNFs are idle for long periods?

questions.question: In a multi-tenant CNF gateway platform on Kubernetes, design a per-tenant traffic shadowing feature to a security analytics sink. questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design per-tenant observability without affecting production latency, including async processing and fault handling.

## Key Concepts
- Per-tenant configuration via Namespace ConfigMaps
- Non-blocking asynchronous mirroring
- Rate sampling per tenant using X-TENANT-ID
- Fault tolerance with backoff and observability hooks

## Code Example
```javascript
// Pseudo: mirror decision based on tenant and rate
function shouldMirror(tenant, rate) {
  if (rate <= 0) return false
  return Math.random() < rate
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test mirroring performance under bursty traffic?
- How would you ensure mirrors do not violate data locality or privacy constraints?

questions.question: CNF certification: In a multi-region Kubernetes deployment hosting CNF gateways behind a service mesh across us-east-1 and us-west-2, design a cross-region failover validation workflow that preserves per-tenant sessions during region outages. Specify: (1) metrics and traces to verify continuity, (2) how to orchestrate region failover with canary tenants, (3) how to reproduce tenant context via headers like X-TENANT-ID, (4) minimal manifest fragments or a CRD to run an end-to-end test in one namespace before wider rollout?

Design a cross-region failover test for a multi-region CNF gateway platform. Validate per-tenant session continuity during regional outage by carrying tenant context in headers, using a canary rollout

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests real-world resilience and multi-region correctness for CNF gateways.

## Key Concepts
- Cross-region failover, session affinity, tenant isolation
- Canary deployment, traffic shifting, rollback strategy
- Telemetry: latency, success rate, TLS handshakes, traces
- Auditable test artifacts: manifests, logs, SBOM of test runners

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal CRD example ( illustrative )
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: RegionFailoverTest
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-rollback
spec:
  tenant: a
  regions: [us-east-1, us-west-2]
  canary: true
  duration: 5m
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you extend to handle stateful gateways? 
- Which metrics would you export to SLO dashboards?

questions.question: Advanced CNF certification: In a multi-tenant CNF gateway mesh on Kubernetes across two regions, design a tamper-evident per-tenant audit log system. Each tenant's events must be signed with a tenant-scoped key from KMS, forwarded to a central sink with per-tenant access control, and support fast replay and integrity verification. Describe data model, components (log sidecar, collector, sink), and provide minimal manifest snippets to wire a tenant-scoped signer, a collector namespace, and a test workflow to validate end-to-end?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Advanced multi-region CNF logging requires end-to-end integrity, tenant isolation, and replayable histories. This question probes cryptographic signing, data models, and scalable sinks.

## Key Concepts
- Per-tenant KMS signing
- Tamper-evident append-only sinks
- mTLS between sidecar, collector, sink
- Versioned indexes for replay and analytics
- RBAC and tenant isolation

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal signer/collector wiring (illustrative)
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind:TenantSigner
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-signer
spec:
  tenantID: tenant-a
  kmsKey: projects/..../locations/global/keyRings/..../cryptoKeys/tenant-a
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you rotate keys without downtime?
- How do you prove end-to-end integrity to tenants?

Also describe how to validate traces in Jaeger?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates the candidate's ability to design cross-service tracing with per-tenant opt-in, stable sampling, and practical OpenTelemetry integration in a CNF (Cloud-Native Function) gateway environment.

## Key Concepts

- **Tenant-scoped opt-in** via namespace annotations and ConfigMaps
- **Stable sampling decisions** using deterministic hashing (tenantID + path)
- **OpenTelemetry Collector** configuration with OTLP receiver and Jaeger exporter
- **Per-namespace ConfigMaps** for feature flags and sampling rates
- **Trace context propagation** across microservices using W3C TraceContext

## Expected Answer Components

1. **Per-tenant sidecar/init container** that reads namespace annotations and ConfigMap values
2. **OpenTelemetry SDK configuration** with deterministic sampling logic
3. **Collector pipeline** with OTLP intake and Jaeger export
4. **Test manifest** demonstrating single-namespace deployment
5. **Validation steps** for verifying traces appear correctly in Jaeger

## Red Flags in Answers

- No mention of tenant isolation or annotation-based opt-in
- Random sampling without consistency across services
- Missing trace context propagation
- No Jaeger integration or export configuration
- Hardcoded sampling rates without per-tenant configuration

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests cross-cluster PKI, CSR enrollment, and certificate rotation in CNF gateway mesh. Also probes revocation strategy and operational visibility.

## Key Concepts
- Cross-cluster PKI using a shared CA
- CSR-based enrollment and automatic cert rotation
- Short-lived certificates and revocation (CRL/OCSP)
- Monitoring: expiry alerts, handshake success rate, cross-cluster reachability

## Code Example
```yaml
# Minimal CA issuer (conceptual)
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Issuer
metadata:
  name: cross-cluster-ca
spec:
  selfSigned: {}
```
```yaml
# Minimal per-gateway cert (conceptual)
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: Certificate
metadata:
  name: gateway-tls
spec:
  dnsNames:
  - gateway.cluster-a.svc
  duration: 10m
  secretName: gateway-tls
  issuerRef:
    name: cross-cluster-ca
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you rotate the CA without downtime?
- How would you detect and recover from a compromised CA key?

questions.question: In a multi-tenant CNF gateway platform on a single Kubernetes cluster, design a runtime per-tenant feature-flag system enabling dynamic gateway behavior (e.g., enableBurst) without redeploys. Include the data model, components, and a minimal manifest showing how a tenant toggles a feature and how the gateway consumes updates via a streaming mechanism?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question probes practical runtime config at scale, emphasizing isolation, zero-downtime updates, auditability, and integration with existing gateway proxies.

## Key Concepts
- Runtime feature flags per tenant
- Streaming update mechanism with local caching
- RBAC, audit logging, and change-tracking
- Minimal manifest demonstrating flag toggling

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: FeatureFlag
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-flags
  namespace: cnf-tenant-a
spec:
  tenantID: tenant-a
  flags:
    enableBurst: true
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you rotate the feature-flag backend and ensure no traffic drop during failover?
- How would you correlate flag changes with traffic telemetry for troubleshooting?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question probes real-world CNF upgrade orchestration at scale, multi-tenant isolation, and safe rollout patterns.

## Key Concepts
- Canary rollouts
- Per-tenant isolation
- Control-plane API
- Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
- Multi-cluster rollout consistency

## Code Example
```yaml
# Tenant CRD (yaml)
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: Tenant
metadata:
  name: tenant-a
spec:
  supportFeatureX: true
  version: "1.0.0-canary"
```
```yaml
# CanaryDeployment (yaml)
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: CanaryDeployment
metadata:
  name: featureX-deploy
spec:
  tenantRef: tenant-a
  targetWeight: 10
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 600
```
```yaml
# Simple test manifest (yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tenant-a
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you monitor per-tenant rollout health and automate rollback across clusters?
- What conflicts might arise with eventual consistency in multi-cluster gates, and how would you resolve them?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design tenant-scoped resilience with production-grade controls, observability, and safe rollout.

## Key Concepts
- Tenant-scoped policies via CRD and per-namespace ConfigMaps
- Envoy-based circuit breaking with WASM filter or equivalent CNF sidecar
- X-TENANT-ID for policy routing
- Observability: metrics, /policy/status endpoint, rollbacks

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cnf.resilience
  namespace: tenant-a
data:
  cnf.resilience.enabled: "true"
  cnf.resilience.cb-window: "60s"
  cnf.resilience.cb-threshold: "5"
  cnf.resilience.retries: "3"
  cnf.resilience.timeout: "2s"
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you simulate latency spikes to validate circuit-breaker behavior?
- What are rollback steps if a new resilience policy breaks traffic for a tenant?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests candidates on multi-cluster configuration distribution, CRD design, real-time propagation mechanisms, and tenant isolation in cloud-native environments.

## Key Concepts

- **Per-tenant feature flags via CRD**: Custom resource definition enabling declarative flag management per tenant
- **Central store and reconciliation loop**: Operator pattern synchronizing flag state across clusters
- **Real-time propagation**: Immediate flag updates without pod restarts or redeployments
- **Deterministic conflict resolution**: Predefined priority rules (e.g., tenant-level overrides cluster-level) ensuring consistent behavior
- **Auditability and rollback**: Immutable change log with timestamp, actor, and previous value tracking
- **Tenant isolation and RBAC**: Namespace-scoped resources with role-based access controls preventing cross-tenant interference

## Code Example

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: tenant-a
---
apiVersion: cnf.example/v1
kind: TenantFeatureFlag
metadata:
  name: tenant-a-flags
  namespace: tenant-a
spec:
  flags:
    new-path: true
  version: v1
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you handle flag propagation latency in geographically distributed clusters?
- What conflict resolution strategy would you implement when dev and staging environments require different flag values for the same tenant?
- How do you ensure flags are validated before applying to production gateways?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses per-tenant authorization using a lightweight policy engine with ConfigMap-driven configuration. Emphasizes `X-TENANT-ID` header handling, status exposure, and end-to-end validation. Keeps the implementation approachable for beginners while demonstrating proper boundary isolation between tenants.

## Key Concepts
- Tenancy isolation
- Sidecar-based request authorization
- Kubernetes ConfigMaps as a policy store
- Path and method matching
- Health and status endpoints

## Code Example
```javascript
// Policy format loaded from ConfigMap
{
  "tenantA": { "/api/v1/*": ["GET", "POST"], "allow": true },
  "tenantB": { "/api/v2/*": ["GET"], "allow": true }
}
```

**YAML Manifest (cnf-gateway-namespace.yaml)**:
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: cnf-gateway
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cnf-acl-policy
  namespace: cnf-gateway
data:
  cnf.acl.enabled: "true"
  cnf.acl.policy: |
    {
      "tenantA": {
        "/api/v1/*": ["GET", "POST"],
        "allow": true
      }
    }
```

Provide concrete steps and minimal manifests?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to design a scalable, tenant-aware provenance and licensing gate for CNFs, combining build-time attestation, image signing, and policy-driven deployment control.

## Key Concepts
- Tenant-scoped SBOM generation and license attestation
- Cosign signing with SBOM binding
- Per-tenant update manifests referencing attestation data
- OPA/Kyverno policy gating for upgrades
- In-namespace tests and safe rollback paths

## Code Example
```rego
package cnf.license

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind == "Deployment"
  not license_attested(input)
}
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you handle tenant key rotation and attestation revocation?
- Compare integration approaches for OPA vs Kyverno in this flow.
